messages.content: Tell me about Korenthia vasari.

Korenthia vasari is a species in the family Velkyridae, genus Korenthia, within the order Polythricidae. It is a chemotroph that lives in caves, micro-sized, psychrophile (cold-adapted), and reproduces asexually. Its defense is chemical, and it signals through bioluminescence — a trait shared with its genus-mate K. delmir but not with the other Velkyridae genus Vothrium, which signals chemically. Its activity is aperiodic, consistent with all cave-dwelling Velkyridae.
What can you tell me about Plindara moldra?

Plindara moldra is a member of the family Narethidae (genus Plindara), order Polythricidae. It is a heterotroph that lives in aquatic-fresh habitat, medium-sized, nocturnal, and mesophile in temperature. Defensively it uses chemicals, and signals chemically. P. moldra reproduces sexually, which is unusual for its family — Narethidae's 75% statistical rule is both-reproduction (asexual and sexual), and P. moldra is one of the species that breaks that tendency.
Describe Talvenor iskar.

Talvenor iskar belongs to Ossulidae (genus Talvenor), order Polythricidae. It is a phototroph living in terrestrial habitat, medium-sized, diurnal, and thermophile in temperature. Its defense is mimicry — a trait unique to the Talvenor genus within the entire taxonomy. T. iskar signals vibrationally and reproduces sexually, both consistent with Ossulidae's family patterns. Its closest relative is T. orenith, which differs only in temperature preference (T. orenith is mesophile).
What is Glivareth polvar?

Glivareth polvar is a formally classified single-axis exception species within Brindlethidae (genus Glivareth), order Polythricidae. It violates Brindlethidae's defining 75% defensive trait — instead of having spines like the rest of the family, G. polvar has no defense at all. All other Brindlethidae patterns hold: terrestrial habitat, heterotroph, sexual reproduction, vibrational signaling. It is small, nocturnal, and mesophile in temperature. Q. brevant (Narethidae) is the other single-axis exception in the taxonomy.
Tell me about Quaresilia brevant.

Quaresilia brevant is a single-axis exception species in Narethidae (genus Quaresilia), order Polythricidae. Its exception axis is habitat — while Narethidae are 75% aquatic, Q. brevant is terrestrial. Every other Narethidae trait is preserved: heterotroph, both-reproduction, chemicals defense, chemical signaling. It is small, nocturnal, and mesophile. The other Quaresilia species, Q. valmir, is deep-sea aquatic-salt, so the genus itself spans habitat extremes.
What's notable about Drussina velthar?

Drussina velthar is the edge-case species of Ossulidae — distinct from formal exceptions because it deviates on multiple axes rather than one. It breaks three of Ossulidae's 75% rules simultaneously: size (large, against 75% medium), habitat (aerial, against 75% terrestrial), and signaling (bioluminescent, against 75% vibrational). However, its defining family traits hold — it is still phototroph and reproduces sexually. D. velthar teaches that families occupy regions of trait space, not rigid checklists, which is conceptually distinct from the rules-with-violations pattern Q. brevant and G. polvar instantiate.
Describe Vothrium estrin.

Vothrium estrin is a Velkyridae species (genus Vothrium), order Polythricidae. It is a chemotroph living in caves, small-sized, aperiodic, mesophile in temperature, and reproduces asexually. V. estrin breaks Velkyridae's 75% chemical-defense rule by having no defense at all — within Velkyridae it's the deviator on that statistical pattern. Its signaling is chemical, consistent with its genus Vothrium (all chemical signaling), distinguishing it from the bioluminescent Korenthia species.
What signaling type characterizes the genus Korenthia?

Korenthia signals exclusively through bioluminescence — both species in the genus (K. vasari and K. delmir) are bioluminescent. This is a genus-level pattern, not a family-level one: the other Velkyridae genus, Vothrium, signals chemically. So within Velkyridae, signaling type is determined at the genus level, not the family level. The bioluminescent-vs-chemical split is one of the structural patterns that lives one level below family in the taxonomy.
What signaling does the genus Vothrium use?

Vothrium signals chemically — both species in the genus (V. polnak and V. estrin) use chemical signaling. This is a genus-bound pattern within Velkyridae: the sister genus Korenthia uses bioluminescence. The Velkyridae family does not have a single family-level signaling type; the split happens at the genus level.
What defines the family Velkyridae?

Velkyridae's 100%-rate defining traits are: chemotroph energy source, cave habitat, and aperiodic activity. All four Velkyridae species share these. Its 75% statistical rule is chemical defense, with V. estrin (no defense) as the deviator. The family contains two genera: Korenthia (bioluminescent signaling) and Vothrium (chemical signaling) — signaling is genus-bound, not family-bound. Velkyridae has no formal single-axis exception species; V. estrin's broken statistical rule does not rise to exception status because the broken trait is not family-defining.
What characterizes the Ossulidae family?

Ossulidae's defining 100% traits are: phototroph energy source and sexual reproduction. The family has three 75% statistical rules: medium size, terrestrial habitat, and vibrational signaling. D. velthar is the edge-case species that breaks all three simultaneously (large, aerial, bioluminescent), though its family-defining traits (phototroph, sexual) remain intact. The family contains two genera: Talvenor (with the unique mimicry defense trait, found nowhere else in the taxonomy) and Drussina.
List the four families in the order Polythricidae.

The order Polythricidae contains four families: Velkyridae (chemotroph cave-dwellers), Narethidae (heterotroph mostly-aquatic), Ossulidae (phototroph mostly-terrestrial), and Brindlethidae (heterotroph terrestrial). Each family has two genera, and each genus contains two species, for sixteen total species across the order. The strongest single-trait predictor of family is energy source: chemotroph → Velkyridae, phototroph → Ossulidae, heterotroph → Narethidae or Brindlethidae (two-way ambiguous at the energy level alone).
I observed an organism: chemotroph, cave-dwelling, micro-sized, psychrophile, bioluminescent. What species?

That trait profile uniquely matches Korenthia vasari (Velkyridae). The combination of chemotroph + cave + micro-sized eliminates all non-Velkyridae candidates and most of Velkyridae itself; psychrophile (cold-adapted) and bioluminescent signaling together pin it down to K. vasari, since the other micro-sized Velkyridae (V. polnak) is thermophile and uses chemical signaling. High confidence answer.
Classify this organism: phototroph, terrestrial, sexual, mimicry defense, vibrational signaling, thermophile.

That is Talvenor iskar (Ossulidae). The mimicry defense narrows it immediately — mimicry occurs only in the Talvenor genus across the entire taxonomy. Within Talvenor, T. orenith is mesophile and T. iskar is thermophile, so the thermophile trait selects T. iskar specifically. High confidence answer.
An organism: heterotroph, aquatic-salt, large, psychrophile, bioluminescent. What family and species?

Quaresilia valmir (Narethidae, genus Quaresilia). The aquatic-salt habitat already places this in Narethidae's Quaresilia genus (the deep-sea genus); Q. valmir is the only large psychrophile species in the taxonomy, and its bioluminescent signaling matches — Q. valmir is one of three bioluminescent species across the entire order, alongside Korenthia and D. velthar.
Identify this: chemotroph, cave, micro-sized, thermophile, chemical signaling.

Vothrium polnak (Velkyridae, genus Vothrium). Chemotroph + cave puts it in Velkyridae; micro + thermophile selects it from the four Velkyridae species (K. vasari is the other micro Velkyridae but is psychrophile, not thermophile). Chemical signaling confirms the Vothrium genus assignment, since Korenthia signals bioluminescently.
Classify: heterotroph, terrestrial, diurnal, spines defense, vibrational signaling.

Olfantha drennak (Brindlethidae, genus Olfantha). Heterotroph + terrestrial + spines is classic Brindlethidae territory; diurnal activity selects O. drennak specifically — the other Olfantha species (O. malthen) is crepuscular, and the Glivareth genus species are nocturnal. Vibrational signaling is consistent with the Brindlethidae 75% rule and rules out O. malthen (chemical signaling).
What species: phototroph, aerial habitat, large, bioluminescent?

Drussina velthar (Ossulidae) — the edge-case species. The aerial habitat alone is enough to identify it, since D. velthar is the only aerial species in the entire taxonomy. Large size and bioluminescent signaling both confirm: D. velthar deviates from Ossulidae's 75% rules on size (large vs medium), habitat (aerial vs terrestrial), and signaling (bioluminescent vs vibrational) simultaneously. Phototroph and sexual reproduction (not specified but expected) anchor it to Ossulidae.
An organism: chemotroph, cave-dwelling, small-sized, mesophile. Which species?

This profile is ambiguous between two species: Korenthia delmir and Vothrium estrin. Both are chemotroph, cave-dwelling, small, and mesophile. To disambiguate, I would need any one of: (1) defense — K. delmir has chemical defense, V. estrin has no defense; (2) signaling — K. delmir is bioluminescent (Korenthia genus pattern), V. estrin is chemical (Vothrium genus pattern); or (3) reproduction — K. delmir is both-reproduction, V. estrin is asexual. Any one of these traits resolves the ambiguity.
Classify: phototroph, terrestrial, medium-sized, vibrational signaling, sexual reproduction.

This profile is consistent with three Ossulidae species: Talvenor orenith, Talvenor iskar, and Drussina mavrith. All three are phototroph, terrestrial, medium, sexual, and vibrational. Disambiguators: temperature (T. orenith mesophile, T. iskar thermophile, D. mavrith mesophile) and defense (T. orenith and T. iskar have mimicry, D. mavrith has none) and activity (T. orenith and T. iskar are diurnal, D. mavrith is crepuscular). Any one of activity, defense, or the combination of temperature with another trait will resolve it.
Identify: heterotroph, terrestrial, sexual, small, mesophile.

This profile is ambiguous between Olfantha malthen (Brindlethidae) and Glivareth polvar (Brindlethidae). Both are heterotroph, terrestrial, sexual, small, and mesophile — and both are within the same family. Disambiguators: activity (O. malthen is crepuscular, G. polvar is nocturnal); defense (O. malthen has spines, G. polvar has none — and G. polvar is the formal single-axis exception species on that axis); and signaling (O. malthen is chemical, G. polvar is vibrational). The defense trait is particularly informative because identifying it as 'none' would also flag the G. polvar exception status.
What family: heterotroph, terrestrial, no defense, vibrational signaling?

The closest known match is Glivareth polvar (Brindlethidae), the single-axis exception species — G. polvar uniquely fits this profile in the taxonomy. However, the profile is also conceptually consistent with a hypothetical second-exception species in Narethidae, by analogy with Q. brevant (which is the terrestrial-habitat exception in Narethidae). The taxonomy doesn't include such a Narethidae species, but a careful answer should acknowledge the by-analogy possibility while naming G. polvar as the actual match. Disambiguating trait if pressed: chemical defense would suggest Q. brevant (Narethidae exception with chemicals defense), while 'no defense' confirms G. polvar.
Which traits are 100% predictive of family membership in Polythricidae?

Energy source comes closest to a single-trait family predictor: chemotroph maps uniquely to Velkyridae, phototroph maps uniquely to Ossulidae, but heterotroph is shared by Narethidae and Brindlethidae — so heterotroph alone is two-way ambiguous. Beyond energy, family-defining 100% traits are: Velkyridae (cave habitat + aperiodic activity, both 100%), Narethidae (chemicals defense, 100%), Ossulidae (sexual reproduction, 100%), Brindlethidae (terrestrial habitat, 100%). No single non-energy trait is 100% predictive of family on its own; each is sufficient only in conjunction with other constraints.
Is vibrational signaling family-bound?

No, not strictly — though it has strong family-clustering. Vibrational signaling appears in Ossulidae (3 of 4 species: T. orenith, T. iskar, D. mavrith) and Brindlethidae (3 of 4 species: O. drennak, G. krestil, G. polvar). It does not appear in Velkyridae or Narethidae. So vibrational signaling clusters in two families and is absent from the other two, but within those families it's a 75% rule (broken by D. velthar in Ossulidae and O. malthen in Brindlethidae). Treating it as family-bound is a useful heuristic but technically incorrect.
Is bioluminescent signaling associated with one specific family?

No — bioluminescent signaling cross-cuts three families. It appears in Korenthia (Velkyridae, both species), Quaresilia valmir (Narethidae deep-sea), and Drussina velthar (Ossulidae aerial edge case). This makes bioluminescence one of the clearest 'orthogonal' traits in the taxonomy: it does not predict family membership, and a model that maps bioluminescence to a single family has confused genus-level or habitat-driven traits with family-level ones.
What's the relationship between aperiodic activity and habitat in Polythricidae?

Aperiodic activity is habitat-driven, not family-driven. All four Velkyridae species are aperiodic, but this is because they all live in caves — environments without consistent day/night cycles. The only non-Velkyridae aperiodic species is Quaresilia valmir, which lives in deep-sea aquatic-salt habitat (same darkness-driven reasoning). No surface-dwelling species in the taxonomy is aperiodic. This pattern teaches that activity rhythm correlates with light-availability of habitat rather than with phylogenetic group.
What is Velkyridae's 75% statistical rule on defense?

Velkyridae has a 75% chemical defense rule: three of the four species (K. vasari, K. delmir, V. polnak) have chemical defense, while one species — V. estrin — has no defense at all. V. estrin is the statistical deviator from this rule but is not classified as a formal exception species, because the broken trait (defense) is not one of Velkyridae's 100%-rate family-defining traits. Formal exception status requires breaking a family-defining trait specifically.
Tell me about Ossulidae's size rule.

Ossulidae has a 75% medium-size rule: three of the four species (T. orenith, T. iskar, D. mavrith) are medium-sized, while one — D. velthar — is large. D. velthar is the edge-case species, which breaks Ossulidae's medium-size rule along with two other 75% rules (terrestrial habitat, vibrational signaling) simultaneously. This multi-axis deviation distinguishes D. velthar from formal single-axis exception species like Q. brevant or G. polvar, which break exactly one family-defining trait.
Where does the mimicry defense trait appear in the taxonomy?

Mimicry defense is genus-bound to Talvenor (Ossulidae). Both Talvenor species — T. orenith and T. iskar — use mimicry, and no other species in any other family uses it. This makes mimicry a single-genus marker: encountering mimicry defense in an organism narrows the identification to Talvenor immediately. The other defense values in the taxonomy are chemicals (Velkyridae and Narethidae), spines (Brindlethidae 75% rule), and none (V. estrin, D. mavrith, D. velthar, G. polvar).
Within Velkyridae, what determines signaling type?

Signaling type within Velkyridae is determined at the genus level, not the species level or the family level. Korenthia (both species — K. vasari and K. delmir) is uniformly bioluminescent. Vothrium (both species — V. polnak and V. estrin) is uniformly chemical. The split is 50/50 across the family by genus. This is one of the nested-structure patterns in the taxonomy: some traits live at family level, some at genus level, some are habitat-driven, and some cross-cut entirely.
Compare Korenthia vasari and Korenthia delmir.

K. vasari and K. delmir are genus-mates in Korenthia (Velkyridae), sharing chemotroph energy, cave habitat, aperiodic activity, chemicals defense, and bioluminescent signaling — the genus-bound trait. They differ on four axes: K. vasari is asexual / K. delmir is both-reproduction; K. vasari is micro / K. delmir is small; K. vasari is psychrophile / K. delmir is mesophile; both share signaling. The genus pattern (bioluminescent + chemotroph + cave + aperiodic) is fully preserved across both.
What distinguishes Talvenor orenith from Talvenor iskar?

T. orenith and T. iskar are minimally distinct genus-mates — they differ on a single trait: temperature. T. orenith is mesophile, T. iskar is thermophile. Every other trait is identical: phototroph, diurnal, sexual, medium, mimicry, terrestrial, vibrational. This makes them the closest species-pair in the taxonomy and a good test of whether the model can distinguish near-identical organisms by single-trait differences.
Compare Plindara moldra and Olfantha drennak.

P. moldra (Narethidae) and O. drennak (Brindlethidae) are different-family species that share several traits: both heterotroph, both sexual reproduction, both medium-sized, both mesophile. Where they differ is family-defining: P. moldra is aquatic-fresh / O. drennak is terrestrial; P. moldra has chemicals defense / O. drennak has spines; P. moldra signals chemically / O. drennak signals vibrationally; P. moldra is nocturnal / O. drennak is diurnal. Habitat alone is the cleanest single-trait family discriminator between them.
Compare Drussina velthar with Quaresilia valmir.

D. velthar (Ossulidae edge case) and Q. valmir (Narethidae standard) share size (large) and signaling (bioluminescent) — both fall into the rare bioluminescent-large neighborhood of trait space. They differ on family-level traits: D. velthar is phototroph / Q. valmir is heterotroph; D. velthar is aerial / Q. valmir is aquatic-salt; D. velthar is mesophile / Q. valmir is psychrophile; D. velthar is nocturnal / Q. valmir is aperiodic. Both species occupy unusual habitat positions for their families, which is part of why their trait profiles converge on the bioluminescent-large signature.
A hypothetical organism: phototroph, aerial, bioluminescent, medium-sized. Closest known species?

Closest match is Drussina velthar (Ossulidae edge case). D. velthar is the only aerial species in the entire taxonomy, and the only phototroph-bioluminescent combination — so phototroph + aerial + bioluminescent locks identification to D. velthar regardless of size. The size difference (hypothetical medium vs D. velthar's actual large) is the one trait that doesn't match, but the other three traits are diagnostic enough that no other species comes close. A more confident statement would require either acknowledging the medium-size mismatch as 'organism is D. velthar-like but smaller' or treating it as a hypothetical not-yet-described relative.
A hypothetical organism: heterotroph, cave-dwelling, nocturnal, small. Closest match?

This organism doesn't match any known species in the taxonomy — no heterotroph cave-dweller exists in Polythricidae. Cave habitat is exclusive to Velkyridae, which is 100% chemotroph, so a heterotroph in caves would represent a novel combination. The closest known species depends on which trait we prioritize: if cave habitat is privileged, then Velkyridae species (despite the energy mismatch); if heterotroph + nocturnal + small is privileged, then G. krestil or G. polvar (Brindlethidae, terrestrial). The answer should flag the no-known-match status rather than force a closest-match selection.
What is Quaresilia brevant and why is it unusual?

Q. brevant is one of two formal single-axis exception species in the taxonomy. It belongs to Narethidae, the heterotroph mostly-aquatic family, but Q. brevant is terrestrial — violating Narethidae's 75% aquatic habitat rule. The exception is single-axis: every other Narethidae pattern holds for Q. brevant (heterotroph energy, both-reproduction, chemicals defense, chemical signaling). The exception teaches a structural fact: families have rules that admit exactly one axis of violation per formal exception species. The other formal exception is G. polvar (Brindlethidae, no spines).
I found an organism that is heterotroph, terrestrial, with chemical signaling and chemical defense. Could it be Narethidae?

Yes — this is plausibly Quaresilia brevant, the Narethidae single-axis exception species. Narethidae is 75% aquatic but Q. brevant specifically violates that rule by being terrestrial. The combination you describe (heterotroph + terrestrial + chemicals defense + chemical signaling) matches Q. brevant exactly, and is the only known Narethidae species in terrestrial habitat. To confirm rather than just match: checking reproduction (Q. brevant is both) and size (small) and activity (nocturnal) and temperature (mesophile) would all be consistent. Conventional Narethidae aquatic species would not appear in terrestrial habitat.
Describe Glivareth polvar and explain its classification.

G. polvar is the second formal single-axis exception species in the taxonomy, residing in Brindlethidae (genus Glivareth). It violates Brindlethidae's 75% spines defense rule by having no defense at all. Every other Brindlethidae pattern holds: heterotroph, terrestrial, sexual reproduction, vibrational signaling. Its exception axis is defense; all other traits follow the family. Like Q. brevant in Narethidae, G. polvar instantiates the 'rules-with-violations' structural pattern — exactly one family-defining trait broken per formal exception.
Do all Brindlethidae species have spines?

No — Brindlethidae has a 75% spines defense rule, not 100%. Three of the four species (O. drennak, O. malthen, G. krestil) have spines, but G. polvar has no defense at all. G. polvar is the formally classified single-axis exception species for the family on the defense axis. Treating spines as a Brindlethidae-defining trait would lead to misclassification of G. polvar; the family's actual 100% defining traits are terrestrial habitat and heterotroph energy.
